chapters.title: Technology is more frightening than it looks
chapters.text:
Around the world, technology is tied to the problems we face. It widens the gap between rich and poor, fuels ethnic conflict, and builds a social split that threatens everyone.

We have to understand how complex technologies work if we want to reverse the trend. That knowledge should not stay locked up among elites and heartless corporations. Otherwise, those groups will keep using the tools in harmful ways.

Our world is becoming a planet of digital addicts, and the shift is so subtle that we often realize how deep we are in only after it is too late. Since this connected world has become part of the physical world, we need to study where it came from and how it runs on purpose.

We cannot settle for using digital tools without thinking. The fact that your phone reminds you about birthdays does not mean you should skip understanding how it pulls the information. Instead of being grateful for the reminder, ask what is in the code and whether it can do things the manual does not mention. To ask those questions, we need to know the digital language and be specific about what we want to know.

This literacy is not about every person learning to write code. Someone can code and still miss what the code hides. Writing code is like working on a factory line. You are one piece of a much larger process. Even if you can describe the whole process, you may not know the secret ingredient that makes the product stick in the market.

Staying open to fresh ways of thinking about emerging technology, while keeping a critical eye, is essential to guiding the change. This summary aims to raise awareness about the harm technology can do and to push everyone to ask sharper questions of the people who run the digital age.

chapters.title: Technology is about more than new tools
chapters.text:
Most people believe technology has made the world more efficient by cutting difficulty and solving problems in record time. Is that the whole picture. The history of computers shows that power ends up in the hands of fewer people. Take computation as an example. Calculations that once needed several mathematicians now happen at the press of a button. A single person can build a project from start to finish at their desk without hearing another view. That narrow window of experience leaves many blind spots. If others had been in the room, someone might have raised the social or ethical questions that would hold the creator to account.

There is more. The computing power of these devices lets them process huge piles of data and return results that end up shaping people is behavior. Some things are doable but should not happen, because they damage how we act and the norms we live by.

Over time, systems and cultures have grown alongside apps backed by private money. Take social media. A phone user can download an app, set up an account, and connect with anyone in the world. A young girl in Lesotho can video chat with someone in Australia. As lovely as that sounds, the exchange is barely moderated. Each can shape the other is local life for better or worse. A cultural exchange might pull one person away from the norms at home. They can also trade economic effects through remote work and money transfers. The world celebrates the good and ignores the bad until a crisis hits.

Meanwhile, the app maker can collect data about both sides of the chat and use it however they like, noble or not. None of this happens with the knowledge of the people on the call. They are just happy to be connected.

Computational thinking has won us over by showing its power first, then muddying us with its complexity, and finally settling into our heads as obvious truth, as James Bridle has written.

chapters.title: Climate change can break our connectivity
chapters.text:
Rising global temperatures stress data systems that already run hot, weakening wireless signals. The faster, broader, and more image heavy our digital life becomes, the more input and energy it needs, which makes it costlier and more wasteful.

As the environment shifts, our expectations shift too, and so does our ability to look ahead. Wireless signals lose range and reliability as temperatures climb. Humidity has the same effect. Electromagnetic waves get heavily dampened as humidity in the air increases, and that humidity has been rising. Are we heading toward a time when electromagnetic waves will be hard to transmit. Do we have a backup plan for this new dark age knocking at the door.

Time and space are pushing back against the systems we built to collapse them. Data centers used about three percent of the world is electrical supply in 2015, housing exabytes of digital data. The energy they use has only climbed since, and will keep climbing as data grows.

Philosopher Timothy Morton has called global warming a hyperobject, meaning it surrounds us but is too vast for any one person to fully grasp. The internet, like nuclear radiation, is also a hyperobject. We cannot fully describe how it changes the fabric of the world.

What we do know is that climate change and technology are bound together. Without solid projections, farming, fishing, flood planning, and defenses would be hard to plan for. The damage to ecosystems threatens our ability to make the precise forecasts civilization depends on. We need to turn our attention to these contradictions.

chapters.title: Wealth is rising but spreading unevenly
chapters.text:
Money has gone digital. Picture a time when cash traveled on camelback from one place to another. Compare that to moving billions of dollars in minutes through blockchain. Faster exchange rates gave rise to high frequency trading. Stock profits come from the speed at which you can pounce on tiny price gaps across markets. Paired with digital communication, finance built a system that earns money off marginal rate changes in split seconds. One company secretly laid fiber optic cables in 2009 to get information faster than anyone else. The project cost three hundred million dollars, and the company saved about seventy five million for every millisecond it beat its rivals.

The result is that those who can cut latency without being seen, like private banks and hedge funds, earn more than any single entity should. Some go further and trade in dark pools, which are trading venues hidden from the wider market and lightly regulated by the Securities and Exchange Commission. In a dark pool, traders can move large volumes without drawing attention from regulators or other investors.

High frequency trading and dark pools are only one example of how financial markets have become more opaque and uneven. Some players make huge profits in milliseconds, widening the gap between rich and poor. Automation also cuts demand for human labor, so more people lose jobs while the owners of the automated systems take in more profit than they should.

Technology grows power and understanding, but without rules it pools power and understanding in the hands of a few. As digital tools wipe out whole professions, we need real options for the people they hurt. Policies should require the winners of automation to fund retraining for the people they replace.

In 1863, telegraphy pioneer Paul Reuter asked that news canisters crossing the Atlantic be dropped into the sea so their contents could be telegraphed before the ships arrived.

chapters.title: Surveillance mixes benefits and worries
chapters.text:
There is a constant tug of war between governments and citizens over information. Things are often called classified or a threat to national security by the people in charge, so they keep the details from the public.

With the new gear that taxpayer money can buy, surveillance has grown to stunning levels in the name of public safety. When the public asks for answers, the CIA and other agencies reply that they can neither confirm nor deny the request. That phrase, called the Glomar response, entered US law and got its name from the mission where it was first used in 1981. Since then, almost every agency has leaned on it. It is a way of refusing to engage, and the default stance of those we are expected to trust, as James Bridle has put it.

Spying on anyone in the world now happens with no real pushback by those in power. You can be tracked through the internet, a phone, a laptop, or any electronic device, and you are likely being tracked. Hackers can take over webcams from afar. What you are reading on your screen is visible to anyone with the means to look. Privacy is gone, and government sits at the center of the game. Each time they are caught, the usual excuse is national security.

The hard truth is that you can have privacy or security, but not both at the same time. The flood of data available to government is so large it has become a problem. Some covert operations were even dropped because there was too much intel on them to keep them quiet.

chapters.title: Fake news is a problem we created
chapters.text:
Content online is copied in many ways. Sometimes a person simply replays what someone else made. Another trick is to take what you find and keep the key terms, so anyone searching for the original lands on your copy. The same idea works on YouTube and Facebook. Creators have used it to drive traffic and build visibility.

The algorithms that suggest what to watch or read never came with a filter to tell original from copy. Some users were handed celebrity status through verified badges. That move happened because the platforms needed more people using them. The faster a product spreads, the better for the bottom line, so who cares if content gets repeated. Since the algorithms stopped spotting duplicates, it became easy to spin up a parody account to hide shady acts.

Today, anyone can broadcast in the name of a public figure on social media without getting flagged. The brand has been split from the content, which lets misinformation and impersonation thrive. People pushing political propaganda can now buy bots that create posts and comment on existing ones. The mix of humans and bots makes it harder to tell which is which.

The bots also make money for the platform owners, so those owners only pay lip service to denouncing fake accounts. For them, it is about the bottom line. We are living through a dark time when the systems we built to make communication and learning faster and easier are being hijacked for harm. Specific content is aimed at people to shape their choices. Politicians pay to play with the public is information and decide what to push to stir society in a chosen direction. The same goes for businesses that want a certain group of buyers.

chapters.title: In closing
chapters.text:
Over the years, technology has reshaped our society, but we barely understand how it works. We are so deep in it that it tells us how to behave and think. We cannot do much without technology, so we need to understand it and how it shapes our lives.

So far, our response to the network we have built has been passive. We need to stop showing up underpowered. Cool gadgets that put information at our fingertips do not guarantee a better life. The opposite can be true. We may have so much information that we cannot think clearly about what to do. We need to see the current moment as doing to our minds what the atomic bomb did to people is bodies when it was first built and used.

The digital age we created is here to stay. We cannot undo what we have made, and we cannot predict what comes next, since we have already crossed the point of no return. We can face reality, speak honestly about what is happening, and act justly in every way we can. We have to put human interest first in everything we do.

Even in the dark, we can choose to build life supporting features into the machines we design. If each person commits to what helps humans, we may find our way back into the light.

A small way to start is to take back control of your phone. Verify the source of any information before you share it. Take social media fasts each week. Stay away from your phone for a few hours, and then for a whole day if you can.
